title: Bread sauce
subtitle: Spiced infused milk, thickened with white bread
excerpt: Whole milk infused with a clove-studded onion and bay, then thickened with fresh white breadcrumbs. The traditional British accompaniment to roast turkey, chicken, and game.
sourceNotes:
Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136. Bread sauce for roast chicken and turkey.

Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845). Bread sauce methods.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to 2 days ahead and reheat gently with a splash of extra milk. Stir frequently to prevent it catching.

glossaryTerms.definition: Steeping aromatics such as a clove-studded onion, bay leaves, or spices in a liquid to transfer their flavour before straining them out.
Pressing whole cloves or other aromatics into an onion or piece of meat so they are held in place during cooking and can be removed cleanly afterwards.

content.text: Bread sauce is an unusual preparation by modern standards: bread as a thickener for a spiced milk sauce. It works because the breadcrumbs soften completely into the milk, producing a texture that is creamy and rich without using cream. The flavour comes entirely from the infusing stage: a long, slow infusion is what separates bread sauce from a glass of warm milk with bread in it.
the onion halves with the cloves by pressing them into the cut surface. Place in a medium saucepan with the milk, bay leaves, and peppercorns.
Heat over very low heat until just below simmering point.
for 30 minutes, keeping the milk just below the boil. Do not rush this step.

Remove the onion, bay leaves, and peppercorns. Add the breadcrumbs and butter. Stir over low heat for 10 minutes until the breadcrumbs have absorbed the milk and the sauce is thick and creamy. Grate in the nutmeg generously. Add the cream if using. Season with salt. Serve immediately, or keep warm with a lid on, stirring occasionally.

items.symptom: The sauce is thin and runny
items.cause: Not enough breadcrumbs, or the infusion time was too short so the milk was cold when the bread was added.
items.fix: Add more breadcrumbs in small amounts, stirring and allowing each addition to absorb before adding the next. The sauce should be thick enough to hold a soft mound.

items.symptom: The sauce has a harsh clove taste
items.cause: Too many cloves, or the infusion went on too long.
items.fix: Six cloves for 500 ml of milk is about right. The clove flavour is background warmth, not dominant. Reduce to 4 next time.

items.symptom: The sauce has a skin forming on top while being kept warm
items.cause: Exposed milk surface cooling and forming a protein skin.
items.fix: Lay a disc of greaseproof paper directly on the surface, or stir every few minutes to prevent skin formation.

items.symptom: The sauce tastes flat and insipid
items.cause: Insufficient infusing time, or the bread was too fresh and soft.
items.fix: The 30-minute infusion is the minimum. Day-old bread absorbs milk more efficiently and adds a slightly more flavourful starch.

content.text: Add 2 tablespoons of double cream at the end for a richer sauce. A pinch of mace alongside the nutmeg gives a more complex spice note. Some older recipes add a tablespoon of dry sherry at the end.

Bread sauce is a medieval British preparation. Bread as a thickener fell out of fashion across European cooking long ago, but this sauce survived in Britain. Victorian cookery writer Mrs Beeton describes it as essential for roast fowl. It remains the traditional accompaniment to roast chicken, turkey, and game birds, and has survived partly through habit and partly because it works so well as a sauce.
